DPmixGPD: Model fundamentals (bulk DPM + GPD tail)

Source: vignettes/model-spec.Rmd
model-spec.Rmd
library(DPmixGPD)

This vignette defines the statistical model underlying DPmixGPD. The three ingredients are:

  1. a Dirichlet process mixture (DPM) for the bulk (central) part of the distribution,
  2. a generalized Pareto distribution (GPD) model for the tail (extremes), and
  3. a proper splicing construction that yields a valid density/CDF on the full support.

Other vignettes (unconditional, conditional, causal) refer back here rather than repeating these definitions.

Bulk model: Dirichlet process mixture

Let y1,…,yny_1,\dots,y_n denote outcomes. DPmixGPD uses a mixture representation yi∣zi=j∼k(yi∣Θj),zi∼Categorical(w1,w2,…), y_i \mid z_i=j \sim k(y_i \mid \Theta_j), \qquad z_i \sim \mathrm{Categorical}(w_1,w_2,\dots), so the bulk density is fDPM(y)=∑j=1∞wjk(y∣Θj). f_{\mathrm{DPM}}(y) = \sum_{j=1}^\infty w_j \, k(y \mid \Theta_j).

A Dirichlet process prior is imposed on the random mixing measure GG underlying the parameters Θj\Theta_j: G∼DP(α,G0),Θj∼iidG. G \sim \mathrm{DP}(\alpha, G_0), \qquad \Theta_j \overset{iid}{\sim} G. Here α>0\alpha>0 is the concentration parameter controlling expected clustering, and G0G_0 is the base measure encoding prior beliefs about kernel parameters (Ferguson 1973).

DPmixGPD supports two equivalent DP representations:

  • stick-breaking (“SB”) (Sethuraman 1994), implemented with a finite truncation at JJ components (Ishwaran and James 2001);
  • Chinese restaurant process (“CRP”), a partition-based formulation (blackwell1973?; Neal 2000).

In SB form (truncated at JJ), vj∼Beta(1,α),w1=v1,wj=vj∏ℓ<j(1−vℓ)(j≥2),wJ=1−∑j=1J−1wj. v_j \sim \mathrm{Beta}(1,\alpha),\quad w_1=v_1,\quad w_j=v_j\prod_{\ell<j}(1-v_\ell)\ (j\ge 2),\quad w_J = 1-\sum_{j=1}^{J-1} w_j.

Tail model: generalized Pareto distribution

For upper-tail modeling, fix a threshold uu and define exceedances x=y−u>0x=y-u>0 for y>uy>u. The GPD density for exceedances is g(x∣σ,ξ)=1σ(1+ξxσ)−1/ξ−1,x>0,σ>0, g(x\mid \sigma,\xi) = \frac{1}{\sigma} \left(1+\xi\frac{x}{\sigma}\right)^{-1/\xi - 1}, \qquad x>0,\ \sigma>0, with support constraint 1+ξx/σ>01+\xi x/\sigma>0. The corresponding CDF is G(x∣σ,ξ)=1−(1+ξxσ)−1/ξ(ξ≠0), G(x\mid\sigma,\xi) = 1-\left(1+\xi\frac{x}{\sigma}\right)^{-1/\xi} \quad (\xi\ne 0), and in the limit ξ→0\xi\to 0, G(x∣σ,0)=1−exp⁡(−x/σ). G(x\mid\sigma,0)=1-\exp(-x/\sigma). This GPD limit result for threshold exceedances is central in extreme value theory (Balkema and Haan 1974; Pickands 1975; Coles 2001).

Splicing: a proper bulk–tail distribution

DPmixGPD combines a bulk model FDPMF_{\mathrm{DPM}} with a GPD exceedance model while ensuring the resulting full distribution integrates to 1.

Let uu denote the tail threshold. Define: pu=FDPM(u),p‾u=1−pu. p_u = F_{\mathrm{DPM}}(u), \qquad \bar p_u = 1 - p_u. The spliced CDF is defined by: F(y)={FDPM(y),y≤u,pu+p‾uG(y−u∣σ,ξ),y>u. F(y) = \begin{cases} F_{\mathrm{DPM}}(y), & y \le u,\\[6pt] p_u + \bar p_u \, G(y-u\mid\sigma,\xi), & y>u. \end{cases} By construction, FF is nondecreasing, right-continuous, and satisfies lim⁡y→−∞F(y)=0\lim_{y\to-\infty}F(y)=0, lim⁡y→∞F(y)=1\lim_{y\to\infty}F(y)=1. The corresponding density is: f(y)={fDPM(y),y≤u,p‾ug(y−u∣σ,ξ),y>u. f(y) = \begin{cases} f_{\mathrm{DPM}}(y), & y \le u,\\[6pt] \bar p_u \, g(y-u\mid\sigma,\xi), & y>u. \end{cases} This “bulk + tail using all observations” Bayesian philosophy matches earlier semiparametric bulk–tail constructions (Frigessi et al. 2002; Behrens et al. 2004; Nascimento et al. 2012).

Posterior computation in DPmixGPD

DPmixGPD implements these models through NIMBLE-backed MCMC. At a high level, posterior inference alternates between:

  1. allocation updates ziz_i (component membership),
  2. component parameter updates Θj\Theta_j,
  3. mixture weight updates (SB/CRP),
  4. tail updates (u,σ,ξ)(u,\sigma,\xi) if GPD = TRUE,
  5. hyperparameter updates (e.g., α\alpha) where stochastic.

All user-facing workflows produce a mixgpd_fit object (or a causal fit object wrapping two mixgpd_fits), which then supports predict(), fitted(), and related S3 methods.

References

Balkema, August A., and Laurens de Haan. 1974. “Residual Life Time at Great Age.” The Annals of Probability 2 (5): 792–804. https://doi.org/10.1214/aop/1176996548.
Behrens, Cibele N., Hedibert F. Lopes, and Dani Gamerman. 2004. “Bayesian Analysis of Extreme Events with Threshold Estimation.” Statistical Modelling 4 (3): 227–44. https://doi.org/10.1191/1471082x04st075oa.
Coles, Stuart. 2001. An Introduction to Statistical Modeling of Extreme Values. Springer. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-1-4471-3675-0.
Ferguson, Thomas S. 1973. “A Bayesian Analysis of Some Nonparametric Problems.” The Annals of Statistics 1 (2): 209–30. https://doi.org/10.1214/aos/1176342360.
Frigessi, Arnoldo, Ola Haug, and Håvard Rue. 2002. “A Dynamic Mixture Model for Unsupervised Tail Estimation Without Threshold Selection.” Extremes 5 (3): 219–35. https://doi.org/10.1023/A:1024072610684.
Ishwaran, Hemant, and Lancelot F. James. 2001. “Gibbs Sampling Methods for Stick-Breaking Priors.” Journal of the American Statistical Association 96 (453): 161–73. https://doi.org/10.1198/016214501750332758.
Nascimento, Fernando Ferraz do, Dani Gamerman, and Hedibert Freitas Lopes. 2012. “A Semiparametric Bayesian Approach to Extreme Value Estimation.” Statistics and Computing 22 (2): 661–75. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11222-011-9270-z.
Neal, Radford M. 2000. “Markov Chain Sampling Methods for Dirichlet Process Mixture Models.” Journal of Computational and Graphical Statistics 9 (2): 249–65. https://doi.org/10.1080/10618600.2000.10474879.
Pickands, James. 1975. “Statistical Inference Using Extreme Order Statistics.” The Annals of Statistics 3 (1): 119–31. https://doi.org/10.1214/aos/1176343003.
Sethuraman, Jayaram. 1994. “A Constructive Definition of Dirichlet Priors.” Statistica Sinica 4 (2): 639–50.
